command.brace_group:
{
\title
{ State in a
[ frame ]
( 006j )
}
\p
{ A state in a frame is just an element of the set of possible worlds
#{ w
\in
W }
in the frame
#{
\mathcal
F = \{ W, R \} }
}
\subtree
[ 006r ]
{
\taxon
{ Example }
\title
{ Examples of states in a frame }
\p
{ To provide some examples of states in a frame let's look at the diagram from Lecture 1 again }
\ltexfig
{ https://q.uiver.app/#q=WzAsNCxbMCwxLCIxIl0sWzEsMCwiMiJdLFsyLDEsIjQiXSxbMSwyLCIzIl0sWzAsMV0sWzEsMl0sWzAsM10sWzMsMl0sWzAsMl0sWzEsMV1d }
{ \[
\begin
{ tikzcd }
& 2 & \\ 1 && 4 \\ & 3
\arrow
[ from=1-2, to=1-2, loop, in=55, out=125, distance=10mm ]
\arrow
[ from=1-2, to=2-3 ]
\arrow
[ from=2-1, to=1-2 ]
\arrow
[ from=2-1, to=2-3 ]
\arrow
[ from=2-1, to=3-2 ]
\arrow
[ from=3-2, to=2-3 ]
\end
{ tikzcd }
\] }
\p
{ There are various states we can assess in this frame diagram }
\ul
{
\li
{
\blockquote
{ 4 is the only state in which
#{
\Box
\bot
}
is true }
\strong
{ Explanation }
: We know this since the local truth for the
\em
{ necessarily }
modality is defined as
##{
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\Box
\phi
\iff
\forall
v :
( wRv )
\to
(
\mathcal
M, v )
\vDash
\phi
}
we know this means that
#{
\Box
\bot
}
is true at a state only if it's true at all successor states. As we can trivially observe that the point
#{ 4 }
has no successors or target nodes, what this means is that we have vacuous truth. }
\li
{
\blockquote
{ 3 is the only state in which
#{
\Diamond
\Box
\bot
\land
\Box
\Box
\bot
}
is true }
\strong
{ Explanation }
: We can break this down into the two conjuncts
\ol
{
\li
{
#{
\Diamond
\Box
\bot
}
- we have both modalities here, we can read this as: there exists some successor for whom all successors are true. Here again our vacuous condition kicks in, 3 indeed has some successor namely 4, and we know from the earlier example that 4 is vacuously true for
#{
\Box
\bot
}
. }
\li
{
#{
\Box
\Box
\bot
}
- this should hopefully be quite straightforward by now as its just equivalent to the above formula given that there only is one successor to 3. }
}
}
\li
{
\blockquote
{ 1 is the only state in which
#{
\Diamond
(
\Diamond
\Box
\bot
\land
\Box
\Box
\bot
)
}
is true }
Given what we know from the prior formula this should be relatively straightforward, we know that entire inner section is only true in state/world 3 hence going back to our definition of local truth for
#{
\Diamond
}
##{
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\Diamond
\phi
\iff
\exists
v:
( w R v )
\land
(
\mathcal
M, v )
\vDash
\phi
}
we clearly only have
#{
(
\mathcal
M, 1 )
\vDash
\Diamond
(
\phi
)
}
where
#{ v = 3 }
. }
\li
{
\blockquote
{ 2 is the only state in which
#{
\Diamond
\Diamond
\top
\land
\neg
\Diamond
(
\Diamond
\Box
\bot
\land
\Box
\Box
\bot
)
}
is true }
Probably makes sense to again break this into the two sides of the conjunct
\ol
{
\li
{ This part seems interesting, if we use our expression again we have
##{
\exists
v:
( w R v )
\land
(
\exists
v':
( v R v' )
\land
\top
)
}
in plain English im fairly certain this is basically just asking which state/world has to successors as the final
#{
(
\mathcal
M, v' )
\vDash
\top
}
is just a tautology, here we technically have 2 states for which this holds, but due to the second conjunct it filters things down to just the state 2.
( I think )
}
\li
{ This seems less interesting as we can just infer it's an exclusion of 1 since thats the only state in which that expression is true. }
}
}
}
}
\subtree
[ 006s ]
{
\taxon
{ Definition }
\title
{ Local truth: some more examples }
\p
{ To expand on some more examples of local truth we can enumerate them below }
##{
\begin
{ align* }
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\nvDash
\Box
p &
\iff
\exists
v
\in
W :
( wRv )
\land
(
\mathcal
M, v )
\nvDash
p \\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\nvDash
\Diamond
p &
\iff
\forall
v
\in
W :
( wRv )
\land
(
\mathcal
M, v )
\nvDash
p \\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\top
&
\iff
\textrm
{ always }
\\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\bot
&
\iff
\textrm
{ never }
\\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\Diamond
\top
&
\iff
\exists
v
\in
W :
( wRv )
\\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\Box
\bot
&
\iff
\
\mathrlap
{ \, / }
{
\exists
}
\ v
\in
W :
( wRv )
\\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\nvDash
\Diamond
\bot
&
\iff
\textrm
{ always }
\\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\Box
\top
&
\iff
\textrm
{ always }
\\
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\vDash
\Diamond
\bot
&
\iff
\textrm
{ never }
\end
{ align* }
}
\p
{ The sort of premise I think when it comes to modalities prepended to
#{
\bot
}
and
#{
\top
}
I think is more or less just analogous to asking if something does or doesnt have a successor. Hence, why
#{
(
\mathcal
M, w )
\nvDash
\Diamond
\bot
}
for example is always true, the line of reasoning being
\ol
{
\li
{ The model "possibly" needs some successor
( where the formula holds )
}
\li
{
#{
\bot
}
definitionally holds nowhere }
\li
{ Hence, it's never the case we have a successor where the formula is true as the formula is just itself
#{
\bot
}
}
}
}
}
}
